input: I'm building a blog backend on a graph database using the runic.ogm library. Define the models: a User node whose email must be unique, and a Post node with a title, an optional summary, a status that is one of draft/published/archived, a created_at timestamp, and a view counter that starts at 0. Show the model classes and a short snippet that creates a user and a post and saves them.
expected_output: Two Node subclasses declared with labels=[...]. User has a primary key and email via Field(unique=True). Post uses a StrEnum for status (relying on the auto-converter, no explicit converter=), a datetime created_at (auto-converter; ideally default_factory with a tz-aware now), summary: str | None = None, and views: int = Field(default=0). Persistence uses a Session(driver) context manager with add()/add_all() then commit(). Driver from create_driver('falkordb', ...) or FalkorDBDriver.

input: Using runic.ogm, I have User and Project nodes. A user can be a member of many projects, and each membership stores a role (owner/member) and a joined_at date on the relationship itself. Show how to model this (including the edge), how to add a membership between an existing user and project, and how to read back the role for a given user/project pair.
expected_output: An Edge subclass declared with type="MEMBER_OF" (or similar) holding role and joined_at. User declares the relationship with Relation(relationship=..., direction="OUTGOING", target="Project", edge_model=Membership) annotated as list[Project]. The membership is created with session.relate(user, User.projects, project, edge=Membership(role=..., joined_at=...)). The role is read back via a traversal with edge_alias and all_with_edges() (using optional=False when filtering on the edge) or a repository cypher query — not a bare attribute, because edge properties live on the edge.

input: With runic.ogm and a User node (fields: name, city, active: bool, joined_at: datetime), write the queries for: (a) all active users whose name starts with 'Al', ordered by name; (b) the number of users in each city; (c) the 10 most recently joined users. Show the exact runic query-builder calls and how you execute each one.
expected_output: (a) session.scalars(select(User).where((User.active == True) & (User.name.startswith('Al'))).order_by(User.name)). (b) a grouped aggregation: select(User).aggregate(count('*').as_('n_users'), group_by='n.city') read via session.all_rows() (rows keyed by 'n.city'). (c) session.scalars(select(User).order_by(User.joined_at, desc=True).limit(10)). Correct execution methods: scalars() for entity lists, all_rows() for aggregation/projection, count() for a single count. No SQLAlchemy/Django-style .filter()/.all()/.label().

input: In a runic.ogm app I have User-[FOLLOWS]->User. (a) How do I load a user together with everyone they follow in a single query (no extra round-trips)? (b) How do I find the friends-of-friends (2 hops) for a given user id? (c) Briefly explain lazy vs eager loading in runic, including what happens in an async session.
expected_output: (a) Eager load with session.get(User, uid, fetch=['following']). (b) Query-builder traversal: select(User).alias('u').where(User.id == uid).traverse(User.following).alias('f').traverse(User.following).alias('fof').return_target('fof') executed via session.scalars (often with .distinct()). (c) Lazy (default) loads on first attribute access and needs a live session (detached access raises DetachedEntityError); eager via fetch= loads in one query. In AsyncSession lazy access raises LazyLoadError, so you must use await session.get(..., fetch=[...]).

input: Using runic.ogm with FalkorDB, I want an Article node that stores: a country code that repeats across many rows, a publication timestamp, a status enum, a 1536-dim embedding for similarity search, and the article's geographic origin (lat/long). Which field types/options should I use so these store natively, and do I need to pass converters?
expected_output: country: str = Field(interned=True) (intern() dedups repeated strings); published_at: datetime (DatetimeConverter auto-applied); status: an Enum/StrEnum (EnumConverter auto-applied); embedding: Vector | None (VectorConverter -> vecf32, auto-applied from the Vector annotation); origin: GeoLocation | None (GeoLocationConverter -> point, auto-applied). No explicit converter= is needed for datetime/Enum/Vector/GeoLocation — they are assigned automatically. Note Vector/GeoLocation/intern are FalkorDB-specific; other backends store raw values.

input: How do I model a polymorphic hierarchy in runic.ogm? I have a Location base type with City and Country subtypes that share common fields (id, title, lat, long) but each add their own. When I query all locations, I want the right concrete subclass back for each row.
expected_output: Declare the base with a shared label and primary_label: class Location(Node, labels=['Location'], primary_label='Location'). Subtypes add their own label while keeping the base label and primary_label: class City(Location, labels=['Location','City'], primary_label='Location'). Subtypes inherit base fields and add their own. Repository(session, Location).find_all() (or select(Location)) returns mixed concrete instances (City/Country) — the decoder picks the class from the node labels; querying select(City) filters to that subtype.

input: In runic.ogm I have a field that should be stored as a JSON string in the graph but exposed as a Python dict on the model. How do I implement a custom type converter and attach it to the Field?
expected_output: Subclass TypeConverter and implement to_graph (dict -> json.dumps str) and from_graph (str -> json.loads dict); optionally set cypher_fn if a Cypher wrapper is needed (not here). Attach it explicitly via Field(converter=JsonConverter()). Explicit converter= always wins over auto-converters. Example: metadata: dict = Field(converter=JsonConverter(), default_factory=dict).

input: I'm using runic.ogm and want the same models to run against Neo4j in production but FalkorDB locally. How do I create the driver for each backend, and which runic.ogm features won't work on Neo4j?
expected_output: Use create_driver from runic.ogm.driver.factory: create_driver('falkordb', host=, port=, graph=) vs create_driver('neo4j', host=, port=, database=, username=, password=) (encrypted=True default). Pass the driver to Session(driver); model/query code is unchanged. FalkorDB-only features: native Vector (vecf32), GeoLocation (point), interned strings (intern); on Neo4j those store raw values. Fulltext/vector search work on Neo4j too but require pre-created named indexes. Indexes are declared on models but created via runic.migrate.

input: How do I declare indexes and constraints on a runic.ogm model — a unique email, a plain range index on a status field, a fulltext index on a body field, and a vector index on an embedding — and how do those actually get created in the database?
expected_output: On the model: email = Field(unique=True), status = Field(index=True), body = Field(index_type='FULLTEXT'), embedding: Vector | None = Field(index_type='VECTOR', default=None). These are declarations only; the OGM does not create indexes. Creation is done by runic.migrate (e.g. IndexManager(graph).create_indexes(Model)) — schema changes belong in migrations, not application code. Mention the runic-migrate skill for the migration workflow.

input: Show me how to write a custom Repository in runic.ogm that has: a method returning Article entities for a given author via Cypher, a method returning a single integer count via Cypher, and a method that returns raw rows (columns + values). What are the right helper calls?
expected_output: Subclass Repository[Article]. find_by_author: self.cypher('MATCH (a:Article {author:$a}) RETURN a', {'a': author}, returns=Article). count_by_status: self.cypher_one('MATCH (a:Article {status:$s}) RETURN count(a)', {'s': status}, returns=int). raw rows: self.cypher_raw('MATCH (a:Article) RETURN a.author AS author, count(a) AS n', {}) -> GraphResult with .columns and .rows. Always parameterize ($name); use write=True for mutations.

input: What's the right way to paginate results in runic.ogm? I want page 3 of Articles (page size 20) filtered to status='published' and ordered by created_at desc, plus the total count for page math.
expected_output: runic.ogm pagination is offset-based via skip/limit; there is no Page object. With the query builder: session.scalars(select(Article).where(Article.status=='published').order_by(Article.created_at, desc=True).skip(40).limit(20)). Or via Repository(session, Article).find_all(skip=40, limit=20). Total via Repository.count() or session.count(select(Article).where(...)). Always order_by a stable key when paging.

input: Using runic.ogm on FalkorDB, how do I run a fulltext search over Article titles/bodies and a vector KNN search over an embedding field, including any filtering? What has to be set up first?
expected_output: Declare Field(index_type='FULLTEXT') on the searchable fields and Field(index_type='VECTOR') on the embedding; create the indexes first (runic.migrate IndexManager.create_indexes(Article)). Then: session.fulltext_search(Article, query='...').where(Article.published == True).limit(10).all(); and session.vector_search(Article, field=Article.embedding, vector=[...], k=5).where(...).all(). Both return session-bound builders with terminal .all(); these are FalkorDB-specific features.

input: I have an Order node in runic.ogm with amount and region fields. I want total revenue and average order value per region, plus the overall completed-order count. Show the query-builder aggregation calls and how to read the results.
expected_output: Per-region grouping uses group_by on a field reference (alias.property), e.g. session.all_rows(select(Order).aggregate(sum_(Order.amount).as_('revenue'), avg(Order.amount).as_('avg_value'), group_by='n.region')) — rows are dicts keyed by 'n.region' plus the aggregate aliases. Overall count: session.count(select(Order).where(Order.status=='completed')). Aggregations are read with all_rows() (not scalars()); name aggregate columns distinctly so they don't collide with the node alias.

input: Show full async CRUD with runic.ogm: open an async session, create a User and a Post and link them, then load the user with their posts. What are the async gotchas?
expected_output: Use AsyncSession(async_driver) as an async context manager. add()/add_all()/delete() are sync; await session.commit(), await session.get(...), await session.relate(...), await session.scalars(...). Link via await session.relate(user, User.posts, post). Critical gotcha: AsyncSession has NO lazy loading — accessing an unloaded relation raises LazyLoadError; you must eager-load with await session.get(User, uid, fetch=['posts']). Query builders from session.query()/fulltext_search()/vector_search() are async (await qb.all()).

input: Can you explain the runic.ogm Session lifecycle and object states? Specifically: how updates get persisted, what commit vs flush vs rollback do, what the identity map guarantees, and what happens if I access a relationship after the session closes.
expected_output: Use `with Session(driver) as session:`. add()/add_all() stage inserts, delete() stages removals, and mutating any field on a loaded entity marks it dirty so the next commit() emits a SET. flush() writes pending changes without ending the logical transaction; commit() flushes then clears tracking; rollback() discards pending state. Identity map: within one session, get() returns the same instance for a given (type, pk). expire()/refresh() re-read from the graph; expunge() detaches. Accessing a lazy relation on a detached/expunged entity (or after the session closes) raises DetachedEntityError — eager-fetch before detaching.

input: In runic.ogm, how does session.relate() behave if the relationship already exists — does it duplicate? How do I update properties on an existing edge, remove a relationship, and model an undirected 'KNOWS' relationship between two users?
expected_output: relate() uses MERGE semantics, so it is idempotent — re-relating the same pair updates the edge rather than duplicating it; pass edge=EdgeModel(...) to set/update edge properties. Remove with session.unrelate(source, field, target). For an undirected relationship, declare the Relation with direction='BOTH' (e.g. contacts: list[User] = Relation(relationship='KNOWS', direction='BOTH', target='User')); accessing it from either endpoint returns the peer. Pass the class-level descriptor (User.contacts) to relate()/unrelate() for type-safe call sites.
